title: Paying the bill
description: End every meal confidently — ask for the bill, split it, and pay by card
context: In Hong Kong you usually have to flag down a waiter to ask for the bill — it won't appear automatically. The phrase 埋單 (maai4 daan1) is what you need. Splitting the bill AA style (AA制) is very common among friends.
cultural_note: Tipping is not a custom in Hong Kong restaurants, and attempting to tip can cause genuine confusion in traditional establishments. Most restaurants add a 10% service charge automatically. What governs the bill is 面子 (min6 zi2, "face"): the person who pays gains social standing, which is why the scramble to grab the bill is performed with such theatrical urgency.

lines.speaker: staff
lines.jyutping: jat1 gung6 ji6 baak3 sei3 sap6 man1.
lines.english: That's two hundred and forty altogether.

lines.speaker: friend
lines.jyutping: AA laa1, jat1 jan4 jat1 baak3 ji6.
lines.english: Let's split it. A hundred and twenty each.

lines.speaker: you
lines.cjk: 好呀。唔該，可唔可以碌卡？
lines.jyutping: hou2 aa3. m4 goi1, ho2 m4 ho2 ji5 luk1 kaat1?
lines.english: Sure. Excuse me, can I pay by card?

lines.speaker: staff
lines.jyutping: ho2 ji5, VISA tung4 Mastercard dou1 dak1.
lines.english: Yes, VISA and Mastercard both fine.
